message:
docs(standards): add FCP2 Requirements Index spec-to-implementation matrix

Add comprehensive requirements index document that maps every section of
the FCP2 specification, README, and connector implementation guide to
their owning implementation and test beads.

Document structure:
- A) Spec Coverage Matrix: 27 sections from FCP_Specification_V2.md
  - Each with Owners, Primary Tests, and Key Requirements
  - Covers: Foundational Axioms, Primitives, Symbol Layer, Zone Architecture,
    Provenance/Taint, Capability System, Mesh Architecture, Wire Protocol,
    Connector Model, Manifest, Automation, Registry, Lifecycle, Security,
    Trust Model, Tailscale, RaptorQ, Offline Access, Agent Integration,
    Observability, Error Taxonomy, Conformance, and all Appendices A-G

- B) README Coverage Matrix: All major README sections mapped to owners
  - Security Invariants, Zone Architecture, Symbol Layer, Mesh Architecture,
    Connector Structure, Security Model, Portfolio, Performance Targets,
    Creating New Connectors

- C) Connector Doc Coverage: docs/fcp_model_connectors_rust.md sections 1-20
  - SDK, Lifecycle, Protocols, Serialization, Zones, Capabilities,
    Receipts, Streaming, Errors, Agent Integration, Manifest, Sandbox,
    Recipes, Registry, Revocation, Leases, Audit, Archetypes, Skeleton

Quick Reference Tables:
- Core Infrastructure (workspace, serialization, IDs, crypto)
- Security & Policy (zones, capabilities, taint, revocation, sandbox)
- Protocols (FCPS, FCPC, sessions, RaptorQ)
- Connector SDK (methods, manifest, state, leases)
- Conformance & Testing (golden vectors, fuzz, interop, E2E, compliance)

Purpose: Engineers can implement FCP2 by following the beads graph without
re-reading the spec/docs. Each entry is a mechanical checklist item.

Closes: flywheel_connectors-1n78.1

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1ah","title":"fcp.postgresql: PostgreSQL Database Connector","description":"# fcp.postgresql - PostgreSQL Database Connector


## Goal
Provide an FCP2-compliant PostgreSQL connector for safe SQL query/transaction execution and optional LISTEN/NOTIFY streaming.

## External surface (NetworkConstraints)
Default deny.
- Network egress MUST be restricted per-operation via manifest `NetworkConstraints` (defined in the connector’s manifest/cap map bead if present).
- Allow only the configured service host(s); HTTPS-only where applicable; deny redirects by default.
- Bound timeouts + response sizes; treat all remote responses as untrusted input; never leak secrets/PII in logs or errors.
- For DB connectors, constrain raw TCP connects to explicit host:port (or unix socket path) only; deny private ranges unless explicitly allowed by policy.

## Overview
PostgreSQL database connector for SQL queries, transactions, and connection pooling.

## Manifest archetypes (closed set)
- `storage`
- `operational`
- `streaming`

> Interaction patterns (non-archetype): Database access (SQL) + (optional) LISTEN/NOTIFY streaming

## Key Operations

### Query Operations
- `pg.query` - Execute parameterized SQL query
- `pg.execute` - Execute non-returning statement
- `pg.batch` - Execute multiple statements
- `pg.prepared` - Execute prepared statement
- `pg.explain` - Explain query plan

### Transaction Operations
- `pg.transaction.begin` - Start transaction
- `pg.transaction.commit` - Commit transaction
- `pg.transaction.rollback` - Rollback transaction
- `pg.transaction.savepoint` - Create savepoint
- Isolation levels: READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE

### Schema Operations
- `pg.schema.tables` - List tables
- `pg.schema.columns` - Get column info
- `pg.schema.indexes` - List indexes
- `pg.schema.constraints` - Get constraints

### Advanced Features
- COPY protocol for bulk load/unload
- LISTEN/NOTIFY for event handling
- Row streaming for large results
- JSON/JSONB operations
- Full-text search support
- PostGIS spatial queries

### Connection Pool
- Configurable pool size
- Connection health checks
- Statement timeout
- Query logging

## Authentication
- Password authentication
- SSL/TLS connections
- Certificate authentication
- AWS IAM (RDS)
- Cloud SQL IAM (GCP)

## Providers Supported
- PostgreSQL (self-hosted)
- AWS RDS/Aurora
- Google Cloud SQL
- Azure Database
- Supabase
- Neon
- CockroachDB (compatible)

## Zone Architecture

<!-- fcp2-single-zone-note -->
**FCP2 single-zone binding note:** A connector instance binds to exactly one `ZoneId` for its entire lifetime.
The *"zones"* described in this section are a **recommended deployment pattern** (run separate connector instances in separate zones with different capability grants/policies) and/or conceptual risk tiers.
The connector MUST NOT multiplex multiple zones internally.

\`\`\`
Zone 0 (Public):     Schema metadata
Zone 1 (Authorized): Read queries
Zone 2 (Private):    Write operations
Zone 3 (Sensitive):  DDL, admin operations
\`\`\`

## Security
- Parameterized queries (no SQL injection)
- Query whitelisting option
- Statement timeout enforcement
- Connection limit enforcement

## Dependencies
- fcp-core, fcp-config, fcp-testkit, fcp-telemetry

## Acceptance Criteria
- The functionality described in this bead is implemented with typed schemas + validation (no unstructured “stringly” interfaces).
- Errors map to the FCP error taxonomy with correct retry semantics and no secret/PII leakage.
- Mock-only unit/integration tests cover success paths and common failures and emit structured JSON logs per `flywheel_connectors-1n78.35`.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1j7","title":"fcp-security: Security Testing and Audit Framework","description":"# fcp-security: Security Testing and Audit Framework

## Overview
Security-focused library providing capability validation, input sanitization, audit logging, and security testing utilities for FCP connectors.

## Capability Token Validation
- Ed25519 signature verification
- Token expiration validation
- Issuer/audience verification
- Capability scope enforcement
- Resource constraint checking
- Constant-time comparison

## Zone Security
- Zone boundary enforcement
- Trust level validation
- Cross-zone access prevention
- Zone hierarchy traversal
- Provenance tracking

## Input Validation
- SQL injection detection
- XSS pattern detection
- Command injection patterns
- Path traversal detection
- SSRF prevention helpers
- Content-Type validation

## Audit Logging
- Security event capture
- Structured audit records
- Tamper-evident logging
- Correlation ID tracking
- PII redaction
- Retention policy support

## Security Testing
- Fuzzing helpers
- Property-based testing utilities
- Security assertion macros
- Vulnerability pattern matchers

## Cryptographic Utilities
- Secure random generation
- Key derivation functions
- Constant-time operations
- Secure memory handling (zeroize)

## Dependencies
- ed25519-dalek, sha2, hmac
- zeroize, rand
- regex (for pattern detection)

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78","title":"[FCP2] MVP: Mesh-Native Core Platform (Rust)","description":"# [FCP2] MVP: Mesh-Native Core Platform (Rust)


## Goal
Build the FCP2 (V2-only) Rust reference implementation: mesh-native, symbol-first, zone-isolated, capability-gated core platform with conformance suite and performance budgets.

## Why this exists
We are building the **FCP V2-only** reference implementation in Rust: a mesh-native, symbol-first, zone-isolated, capability-gated connector platform. The deliverable is the **system described by the FCP V2 specification + README + connector model guide**, implemented as a set of Rust crates and CLIs that can power a library of production connectors.

This epic is the parent for the concrete subsystem issues that implement the MVP conformance target.

## Non-negotiable project constraints
- **NO backwards compatibility:** We do **not** support FCP1/JSON-RPC compatibility modes, translators, or hybrid shims. If migrating from anything older, do a clean cutover.
- **No unsafe Rust:** `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` everywhere.
- **Ultra-optimized defaults:** low latency, low allocation churn, bounded memory spikes, predictable performance.
- **Security is mechanical:** enforcement must not depend on agent prompts.

## The three axioms (what everything must preserve)
1. **Universal Fungibility:** Every durable thing is a content-addressed object that is symbol-addressable (RaptorQ). Even when transported via a control-plane stream, it must still have a canonical object form.
2. **Authenticated Mesh:** Tailscale is transport and identity (node IDs, ACL tags). FCP must assume the tailnet is authenticated but still enforce crypto + policy.
3. **Explicit Authority:** No ambient authority. All authority chains back to the owner root key via verifiable signatures.

## MVP conformance target (ship this first)
This epic is complete when we have a V2-only platform that supports, at minimum:
- **Canonical object format**: deterministic CBOR with schema-hash prefix; stable schema IDs/hashes.
- **Security object addressing**: keyed ObjectId derivation bound to zone + schema for security-relevant objects.
- **Zone crypto**: ZoneKeyManifest distribution (HPKE sealed boxes) and rotation.
- **Capability system**: COSE_Sign1 (CWT) CapabilityTokens with grant_object_ids verification; aud_binary binding for risky ops.
- **Revocation**: revocation objects + head/seq freshness semantics + policy (Strict/Warn/BestEffort).
- **Receipts**: OperationIntent + OperationReceipt for exactly-once semantics where required.
- **Audit**: AuditEvent chain + AuditHead + ZoneCheckpoint + DecisionReceipt for explainability.
- **Wire protocols**:
  - **FCPS** symbol-native data plane (frame format, per-symbol AEAD, per-session MACs).
  - **FCPC** control plane (ordered, backpressured framing of canonical control-plane objects).
- **RaptorQ + chunking**: symbol encoding/decoding for objects; ChunkedObjectManifest for large objects.
- **Stores**: object store + symbol store + placement policy + background repair loop + retention/GC roots.
- **MeshNode**: peer discovery via Tailscale, routing, admission control, and distributed coordination primitives (leases).
- **Sandbox + egress proxy**: enforce NetworkConstraints and deny-by-default egress.
- **Connector SDK + manifest**: connectors implement standard methods and embed a manifest; SDK makes this ergonomic.
- **Conformance harness**: golden vectors, fuzz targets, and mechanical compliance checks.

## Definition of Done (quality gates)
- `cargo check --all-targets`
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo test` (including conformance tests)
- Fuzz targets exist for the spec-required surfaces (frame parsing, handshake transcripts, token verification, key manifests).
- Benchmarks exist for the hot paths (ObjectId, CBOR canonicalization, FCPS parse, RaptorQ decode).

## Work breakdown (child issues)
This epic is intentionally decomposed into subsystem issues (types/crypto/protocol/store/mesh/sandbox/sdk/conformance). Each child issue must be self-contained with:
- a crisp scope and non-goals
- concrete deliverables
- acceptance criteria and test strategy
- performance and security considerations

## Success Criteria
- The MVP system can execute a V2 connector end-to-end under zone/capability enforcement, producing receipts and audit events.
- Conformance suite is green (golden vectors + fuzz targets exist and run).
- No unsafe code; performance budgets are tracked and not regressing.

-{"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.1","title":"[FCP2] Requirements Index: Spec-to-Implementation Matrix (V2-only)","description":"# [FCP2] Requirements Index: Spec-to-Implementation Matrix (V2-only)


## Goal
Maintain a complete, V2-only mapping from spec/docs to owning implementation+test beads so the whole system can be built without re-reading the docs.

## Purpose
This bead is the **single source of truth** mapping from:
- `FCP_Specification_V2.md`
- `README.md`
- `docs/fcp_model_connectors_rust.md`

…to the **owning Beads** (implementation + tests).

If we keep this up to date, engineers should be able to implement FCP2 by following the Beads graph **without rereading the spec/docs**.

## How to Use This Bead
- Treat each entry as a **mechanical checklist** item.
- When you discover a missing requirement, update this matrix first, then:
  - create the missing owning bead(s), or
  - expand the owning bead’s scope/acceptance criteria/tests.

## Conformance Profiles (Spec §25)
- **MVP Profile:** must ship first and is the default target for the reference implementation.
- **Full Profile:** post-MVP hardening features (enabled by explicit configuration/policy).

This matrix labels some items as **MVP** or **Full**. Many are shared.

## V2-Only Clarifications (Spec §26)
- FCP2 makes **no backwards-compat guarantees** with FCP1.
- No hybrid translator layer.
- Clean cutover only.

## Pricing / Cost Tracking Clarification (Connector-facing)
- Connectors MUST emit **usage metrics**.
- We do **not** embed hard-coded pricing tables in connectors (pricing changes frequently).
- If cost estimates are supported, they are explicit and policy/ops-owned (e.g., `CostEstimate`).

---

# A) Spec Coverage Matrix — `FCP_Specification_V2.md`

Below, each chapter lists:
- **Owners**: implementation beads responsible for building it.
- **Primary Tests/Conformance**: golden vectors, fuzz, interop, E2E, compliance runner.
- **Notes / Key Requirements**: the “sharp edges” we must not forget.

## Spec: Conformance Language
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21` (overall conformance)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.1` (CDDL + golden vectors)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- RFC2119 language interpretation.
- Anything labeled NORMATIVE is part of the interop/security contract.

## Spec §1: Introduction (informative)
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.1` (this matrix)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- n/a

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Establishes that FCP is object-native, mesh-authenticated, explicit authority.

## Spec §2: Foundational Axioms
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.3` (canonical serialization)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.4` (IDs + headers)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.11` (audit/checkpoints as roots)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.1` (golden vectors + CDDL)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Persisted/audited/mirrored things must be representable as canonical mesh objects.
- Mesh is authenticated; authority is explicit and provable.

## Spec §3: Foundational Primitives
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.4` (ObjectId/EpochId/ZoneId/Schemas/ObjectHeader)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.3` (canonical CBOR + schema hash)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.5` (crypto primitives)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.1` (golden vectors + CDDL)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.2` (fuzz: parsers/decoders/verifiers)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Canonical identifier formats; reject non-canonical forms (no silent normalization).
- ObjectHeader rules + retention semantics and root pointers.
- HPKE sealed box shape requirements (used by zone crypto).
- ZoneCheckpoint is the “root pointer” (GC/security).

## Spec §4: Symbol Layer
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.12` (FCPS framing + SymbolEnvelope AEAD)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.24` (session auth, key schedule, anti-replay)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.14` (RaptorQ + chunking)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.17.2` (multipath + transport priority)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.1` (golden vectors)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.2` (fuzz)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.3` (interop)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Session MACs required for throughput; Poly1305 key reuse rules.
- MTU safety and explicit frame size limits.
- Multipath delivery must respect ZoneTransportPolicy.

## Spec §5: Zone Architecture
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.26` (ZoneDefinition/ZonePolicy/roles/resource objects/reason codes)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.16` (Zone ↔ Tailscale tag mapping, port gating)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.6` (zone crypto key distribution)
- `flywheel_connectors-6o25.5` (optional MLS/TreeKEM PCS zones)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.26.1` (policy decision vectors)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Zone hierarchy + mapping to Tailscale ACL tags.
- ZoneDefinitionObject + ZonePolicyObject must be enforceable and auditable.

## Spec §6: Provenance and Taint Tracking
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.8` (provenance model, taint propagation, elevation protocol)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.11` (DecisionReceipt evidence + explainability)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.36.4` (E2E taint/approval flow)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Taint propagation is mechanical.
- Elevation/declassification requires ApprovalTokens and receipts.

## Spec §7: Capability System
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.7` (CapabilityObject + COSE_Sign1 tokens)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.18` (egress proxy + NetworkConstraints enforcement)
- `flywheel_connectors-kt9r` (credential injection model + zeroize discipline)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.26` (roles/bundles, placement policy)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.5` (mechanical compliance runner)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.3` (interop tokens/handshake)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Capability IDs MUST NOT encode hostnames/ports; use `network_constraints`.
- Risky/Dangerous requires holder binding proof where applicable.
- Egress proxy credential injection is normative for strict/moderate.

## Spec §8: Mesh Architecture
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.17` (mesh node baseline)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.17.3` (admission control + anti-amplification + quarantine enforcement)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.17.5` (gossip baseline)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.25` (trust/quorum/degraded mode semantics)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.4` (system E2E harness)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Admission control is mandatory; symbol request bounding is the big DoS surface.
- Distributed state must be convergent; quarantine prevents storage poisoning.

## Spec §9: Wire Protocol
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.12` (FCPS)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.13` (FCPC)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.24` (sessions)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.17.4` (SymbolRequest bounding + targeted repair plumbing)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.1` (golden vectors)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.2` (fuzz)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.3` (interop)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- SymbolRequest bounding and anti-amplification rules are NORMATIVE.
- Control-plane object model is canonical; retention classes are enforced.
- SimulateRequest/Response + CostEstimate are NORMATIVE control-plane objects; SDK support: `flywheel_connectors-1n78.20.2`; tests: `flywheel_connectors-r1sl`.

## Spec §10: Connector Model
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.20` (SDK: standard method surface, typed schemas)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.33` (connector state: persisted, snapshot, singleton-writer leases)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.34` (leases)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.31` (provisioning automation patterns)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.5` (mechanical compliance runner)
- `flywheel_connectors-h32` (connector test standards)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Connectors implement standard methods.
- Stateful connectors externalize canonical state to mesh.

## Spec §11: Connector Manifest
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.19` (manifest TOML + embedding + validation)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.18` (sandbox profiles + egress enforcement)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.27` (supply chain verification, mirroring)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.5` (static + dynamic checks)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Manifests extractable without execution (`--manifest`).
- Sandbox profiles + network guard are enforceable.

## Spec §12: Automation Recipes
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.31` (recipe model + provisioning interface)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- Connector-specific provisioning test beads (per connector epic)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Minimize human prompts; automate OAuth/webhook setup.

## Spec §13: Registry and Supply Chain
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.27` (verify/install/mirror)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.27.2` (optional transparency/TUF/sigstore)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.9` (revocation of binaries)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.27.1` (registry verification + mirroring tests)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Registries are sources, not dependencies.
- Binaries are content-addressed and can be symbol-distributed.

## Spec §14: Lifecycle Management
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-y1q8` (connector activate/update/rollback + crash-loop detection)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.9` (revocation objects/heads/freshness)
- `flywheel_connectors-7isb` (checkpoint/frontier advancement protocol)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.27` (supply chain + install/mirror + update/rollback artifacts)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-dlfp` (lifecycle activation/update/rollback tests)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.36.3` (revocation E2E)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Activation MUST fail closed unless verification + policy ceilings + sandbox creation succeed.
- Revocations enforced before use; freshness policy is mechanical.
- Fork detection halts advancement unless explicit degraded mode.

## Spec §15: Device-Aware Execution
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.34` (leases)
- `flywheel_connectors-wwq8` (DeviceProfile schema + fitness)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.17.6` (execution planner)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.34.1` (lease semantics tests)
- `flywheel_connectors-u4ej` (DeviceProfile unit tests)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Leases are fenced locks; quorum rules are explicit.
- Planner decisions are explainable and deterministic.

## Spec §16: Computation Migration (Full)
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-6o25.4` (migration protocol)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-fbql` (migration tests)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Checkpoint objects + lease handoff before resume.
- Fail closed under partition to prevent double execution.

## Spec §17: Security Model
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.18` (sandbox/egress)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.25` (trust/quorum/degraded mode)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.27` (supply chain)
- `flywheel_connectors-6o25.*` (full-profile hardening)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.2` (fuzz/adversarial)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Defense-in-depth; threshold secrets; source diversity.

## Spec §18: Trust Model and Byzantine Assumptions
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.25` (n/f assumptions, quorum rules, degraded mode semantics)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.25.*` (tests as defined under that bead)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Explicit degraded mode; fail-closed semantics for high-safety tiers.

## Spec §19: Tailscale Integration
Owners:
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.16` (tailscale client + tag mapping)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.32` (device enrollment/removal)
- `flywheel_connectors-1n78.17.2` (DERP/Funnel policy controls)

Primary Tests/Conformance:
- `flywheel_connectors-wyz0` (tailscale integration unit tests)

Notes / Key Requirements:
- Enrollment/removal is normative; removal triggers revocations.

## Goal
Maintain a complete, V2-only mapping from spec/docs to owning implementation+test beads so the whole system can be built without re-reading the docs.

## Purpose
This bead is the **single source of truth** mapping from:
- `FCP_Specification_V2.md`

…to the **owning Beads** (implementation + tests).

## How to Use This Bead
- Treat each entry as a **mechanical checklist** item.

  - create the missing owning bead(s), or
  - expand the owning bead’s scope/acceptance criteria/tests.

## V2-Only Clarifications (Spec §26)
- FCP2 makes **no backwards-compat guarantees** with FCP1.
- No hybrid translator layer.
- Clean cutover only.

- Connectors MUST emit **usage metrics**.
- We do **not** embed hard-coded pricing tables in connectors (pricing changes frequently).
- If cost estimates are supported, they are explicit and policy/ops-owned (e.g., `CostEstimate`).

In scope:
- How to install and run APR rounds.
- How to include the implementation doc periodically.
- How to run remotely (Oracle serve mode) when the dev box has no browser.
- How to integrate APR feedback back into the docs in a disciplined way.

Out of scope:
- Any “backwards compatibility” plan with FCP1. FCP2 is a clean cutover.
- Installing Node/npm tooling as a requirement for the shipped platform.

## Why this matters
The spec is large and security-sensitive. Iterative AI review is a force multiplier, but only if:
- we can repeat it reliably,
- we can reproduce what changed and why,
- and we integrate feedback in a way that preserves the spec’s internal consistency.

## Baseline assumptions
- You have a GPT Pro account available for Oracle/APR.
- You can authenticate in a browser for the initial APR run.
- You can run the workflow either locally or with Oracle serve mode over Tailscale.

## Workflow configuration
APR workflow file: `.apr/workflows/fcp.yaml`

Expected config (informational):
- Documents:
  - `readme`: `README.md`
  - `spec`: `FCP_Specification_V2.md`
  - `implementation`: `docs/fcp_model_connectors_rust.md`

## Setup (one-time)
Install APR:
```bash
curl -fsSL "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Dicklesworthstone/automated_plan_reviser_pro/main/install.sh" | bash
```

Install Oracle (browser automation):
```bash
npm install -g @steipete/oracle
```

Note: Oracle is tooling only; it is not part of the FCP2 runtime distribution.

## Running revision rounds
First round (requires manual ChatGPT login):
```bash
apr run 1 --login --wait
```

Subsequent rounds:
```bash
apr run 2
apr run 3 --include-impl  # include implementation doc every 3–4 rounds
```

Inspect status and output:
```bash
apr status
apr show 5
```

## Remote/SSH setup (Oracle “serve mode”)
Use this if your dev box is remote (SSH) and cannot open a local browser.

On your local machine (with browser):
```bash
oracle serve --port 9333 --token "your-secret-token"
```

On the remote server:
```bash
export ORACLE_REMOTE_HOST="100.x.x.x:9333"  # local machine’s Tailscale IP
export ORACLE_REMOTE_TOKEN="your-secret-token"

# test connection
oracle -p "test" -e browser -m "5.2 Thinking"

# now APR works normally
apr run 1
```

Important: use port `9333` (not `9222`) to avoid clashing with Chrome DevTools.

## Integration discipline (how to apply feedback)
When APR produces feedback for round N:

1) Prime the coding agent with full context (don’t assume partial memory):
- Read ALL of `AGENTS.md` and `README.md`.
- Read `FCP_Specification_V2.md` and `docs/fcp_model_connectors_rust.md`.

2) Integrate feedback carefully:
- Treat GPT output as suggestions; validate each claim against the spec’s goals.
- Prefer “mechanical enforcement” changes over editorial prose.
- Ensure changes do not introduce FCP1 compatibility shims.

3) Harmonize docs in this order:
- Update `README.md` first for the human-facing story.
- Update `docs/fcp_model_connectors_rust.md` to keep connector authoring aligned.
- Update `FCP_Specification_V2.md` last (authoritative) to ensure correctness.

4) Commit in logical groupings with clear messages.

## Useful APR commands
```bash
apr status          # check Oracle sessions
apr list            # list workflows
apr history         # show revision history
apr diff 4 5        # compare rounds 4 and 5
apr stats           # convergence analytics
apr integrate 5 -c  # copy integration prompt to clipboard
```

## Key files
- `FCP_Specification_V2.md` — main protocol specification
- `docs/fcp_model_connectors_rust.md` — Rust implementation guide
- `.apr/workflows/fcp.yaml` — workflow configuration
- `.apr/rounds/fcp/round_N.md` — GPT Pro output per round

## Acceptance Criteria
- A new contributor can run APR rounds without consulting README.md.
- The workflow notes explicitly keep FCP2 V2-only (no FCP1 compatibility work).
- The workflow documents remote mode and integration discipline.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.10","title":"[FCP2] Exactly-Once Semantics: OperationIntent + OperationReceipt + Idempotency","description":"# [FCP2] Exactly-Once Semantics: OperationIntent + OperationReceipt + Idempotency

## Goal
Provide exactly-once semantics for external side effects (where required) by combining:
- an idempotency key
- an OperationIntent pre-commit object
- an OperationReceipt object that is returned on retries

## Required behavior (spec-aligned)
- For Strict idempotency and for Risky/Dangerous operations:
  - MeshNode MUST store OperationIntent (Required retention) **before** executing external side effects.
  - OperationIntent SHOULD bind to the execution lease fencing token (`lease_seq`) when applicable.
  - OperationReceipt MUST reference the OperationIntent.
  - Retries with the same idempotency key must return the prior receipt without re-executing.

## Subtasks
- Define OperationIntent and OperationReceipt schemas (include refs/evidence fields; include optional upstream idempotency handle).
- Define storage/indexing model to look up receipts by idempotency key.
- Integrate with audit: emitting audit events on intent and receipt.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Unit tests: retry returns same receipt without re-execution.
- Fault injection tests: crash between intent and receipt is handled safely (detect and reconcile incomplete intents).

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.11","title":"[FCP2] Audit + Explainability: AuditEvent Chain, AuditHead, ZoneCheckpoint, DecisionReceipt","description":"# [FCP2] Audit + Explainability: AuditEvent Chain, AuditHead, ZoneCheckpoint, DecisionReceipt

## Goal
Make all security-relevant actions tamper-evident and explainable. Every denial MUST be traceable to specific evidence. Every security-critical action MUST be logged.

## Background and Motivation

### Why Audit Matters for FCP2
Audit is not optional logging—it is a **security invariant**. Without tamper-evident audit:
- Incident response is guesswork
- Compliance is unverifiable  
- "Why was this denied?" has no answer
- Compromised nodes can hide their actions

FCP2 audit is **hash-linked and quorum-signed by construction**, making it:
- Mechanically verifiable (any node can detect tampering)
- Fork-detectable (multiple heads for same epoch trigger alerts)
- Explainable (every denial includes evidence ObjectIds)

### Design Principles
1. **Tamper-evident by construction**: Hash-linked chains with monotonic seq
2. **Explainable by default**: DecisionReceipt includes evidence ObjectIds
3. **Efficient sync**: Monotonic seq enables O(1) freshness comparison
4. **Byzantine-resilient**: Quorum-signed heads under n/f model

## Normative Types (MUST implement per spec §23)

### AuditEvent (NORMATIVE)
```rust
/// Audit event (NORMATIVE)
/// 
/// Append-only, hash-linked audit chain per zone.
/// Each event links to its predecessor via `prev` and carries
/// a monotonic `seq` for O(1) freshness comparison.
pub struct AuditEvent {
    pub header: ObjectHeader,
    /// Correlation ID for request tracing (16 bytes)
    pub correlation_id: [u8; 16],
    /// Optional full trace context (NORMATIVE when present in InvokeRequest)
    /// Enables stitching mesh routing, connector execution, receipts, and audit together.
    pub trace_context: Option<TraceContext>,
    /// Event type (e.g., "secret.access", "capability.invoke", "elevation.granted")
    pub event_type: String,
    /// Actor who triggered the event
    pub actor: PrincipalId,
    /// Zone where event occurred
    pub zone_id: ZoneId,
    /// Connector ID (if applicable)
    pub connector_id: Option<ConnectorId>,
    /// Operation ID (if applicable)
    pub operation: Option<OperationId>,
    /// Capability token JTI (if applicable)
    pub capability_token_jti: Option<Uuid>,
    /// Request object ID (if applicable)
    pub request_object_id: Option<ObjectId>,
    /// Result object ID (if applicable)
    pub result_object_id: Option<ObjectId>,
    /// Previous event in chain (hash link) - enables tamper detection
    pub prev: Option<ObjectId>,
    /// Monotonic chain sequence number (NORMATIVE)
    /// Enables O(1) freshness comparison: seq_a > seq_b ⟹ a is fresher than b.
    pub seq: u64,
    /// When event occurred (Unix timestamp)
    pub occurred_at: u64,
    /// Signature by executing node
    pub signature: Signature,
}
```

### AuditHead (NORMATIVE)
```rust
/// Audit head checkpoint (NORMATIVE)
/// 
/// Quorum-signed checkpoint of the audit chain head.
/// Enables fast sync without full chain traversal.
pub struct AuditHead {
    pub header: ObjectHeader,
    /// Zone this head covers
    pub zone_id: ZoneId,
    /// Head event ObjectId (tip of the chain)
    pub head_event: ObjectId,
    /// Sequence number of head_event (NORMATIVE)
    /// Enables O(1) freshness comparison without chain traversal.
    pub head_seq: u64,
    /// Fraction of expected nodes contributing (0.0-1.0)
    pub coverage: f64,
    /// Epoch this head was checkpointed
    pub epoch_id: EpochId,
    /// Quorum signatures from nodes (Byzantine-resilient)
    pub quorum_signatures: Vec<(TailscaleNodeId, Signature)>,
}
```

### ZoneCheckpoint (NORMATIVE)
```rust
/// Zone checkpoint for fast sync (NORMATIVE)
///
/// Quorum-signed checkpoint of zone state for efficient synchronization.
/// Acts as the single GC root (so reachability GC is well-defined).
/// Nodes can compare checkpoints to quickly determine staleness.
pub struct ZoneCheckpoint {
    pub header: ObjectHeader,
    pub zone_id: ZoneId,
    
    // Enforceable heads (NORMATIVE):
    pub rev_head: ObjectId,           // Revocation chain head
    pub rev_seq: u64,                 // Revocation chain seq
    pub audit_head: ObjectId,         // Audit chain head
    pub audit_seq: u64,               // Audit chain seq
    
    // Policy/config heads (NORMATIVE):
    pub zone_definition_head: ObjectId,
    pub zone_policy_head: ObjectId,
    pub active_zone_key_manifest: ObjectId,
    
    /// Monotonic checkpoint sequence (NORMATIVE; per-zone)
    pub checkpoint_seq: u64,
    pub as_of_epoch: EpochId,
    /// Quorum-signed (Byzantine-resilient under n/f model)
    pub quorum_signatures: Vec<QuorumSignature>,
}
```

### DecisionReceipt (NORMATIVE)
```rust
/// Decision receipt for explainable allow/deny (NORMATIVE)
///
/// Content-addressed "why allowed/denied" record with stable reason_code
/// and evidence ObjectIds. This is what powers `fcp explain`.
pub struct DecisionReceipt {
    pub header: ObjectHeader,
    /// The request that was evaluated
    pub request_object_id: ObjectId,
    /// The decision (Allow or Deny)
    pub decision: Decision,
    /// Stable reason code for programmatic handling
    /// Examples: "capability.insufficient", "checkpoint.stale_frontier",
    /// "revocation.stale_frontier", "taint.public_input_dangerous"
    pub reason_code: String,
    /// Evidence ObjectIds that support this decision
    /// For denials: the missing capability, stale checkpoint, etc.
    /// For allows: the authorizing capability objects, approval tokens
    pub evidence: Vec<ObjectId>,
    /// Optional human-readable explanation
    pub explanation: Option<String>,
    /// Signature by evaluating node
    pub signature: Signature,
}

pub enum Decision {
    Allow,
    Deny,
}
```

### TraceContext (NORMATIVE when present)
```rust
/// Distributed trace context for end-to-end observability (NORMATIVE when present)
/// W3C Trace Context compatible.
pub struct TraceContext {
    /// 16-byte trace id (unique per logical request)
    pub trace_id: [u8; 16],
    /// 8-byte span id (unique per span within trace)
    pub span_id: [u8; 8],
    /// Sampling/flags (W3C Trace Context compatible)
    pub flags: u8,
}
```

## Required Audit Events (NORMATIVE)

The following events MUST be recorded:

| Event Type | Trigger | Required Fields |
|------------|---------|-----------------|
| `secret.access` | Secret reconstruction/use | secret_id, purpose, accessor |
| `capability.invoke` | Risky/Dangerous operation | capability_token_jti, operation |
| `elevation.granted` | Integrity elevation approved | approval_token_id, target_zone |
| `declassification.granted` | Confidentiality downgrade | from_zone, to_zone, object_ids |
| `zone.transition` | Data crosses zone boundary | from_zone, to_zone, object_id |
| `revocation.issued` | New revocation published | revocation_object_id, scope |
| `security.violation` | Policy violation detected | reason_code, evidence |

## Quorum Rules (NORMATIVE)

**Default:** CriticalWrite requires n - f signatures (see V2 spec §18).

- Nodes MUST refuse to advance AuditHead if quorum is not satisfied
- Exception: explicit degraded mode with owner acknowledgment
- Degraded mode MUST be logged and time-bounded

## Fork Detection (NORMATIVE)

Nodes discovering multiple heads for the same epoch MUST:
1. **Log the fork event** with both head ObjectIds
2. **Refuse to advance** until reconciled
3. **Alert owner** for manual resolution
4. **Emit security audit event** of type `audit.fork_detected`

## TraceContext Propagation (NORMATIVE when present)

When `InvokeRequest.trace_context` is present:
- It MUST be preserved through to `AuditEvent.trace_context`
- It MUST be preserved through to `OperationReceipt` (via header refs)
- This enables end-to-end distributed tracing across mesh routing, connector execution, receipts, and audit

## Acceptance Criteria

### Unit Tests
- [ ] Audit chain hash linking works (verify prev linkage)
- [ ] Monotonic seq invariants hold (seq always increases)
- [ ] Decision receipt includes evidence and stable reason code
- [ ] Fork detection identifies conflicting heads

### Property Tests
- [ ] Monotonic seq invariants under concurrent appends
- [ ] Hash chain integrity under random event insertion
- [ ] Quorum signature verification

### Integration Tests
- [ ] End-to-end trace context propagation
- [ ] DecisionReceipt enables `fcp explain` without guessing
- [ ] Fork detection triggers alert

### Benchmarks
- [ ] Audit event serialization
- [ ] Quorum signature verification
- [ ] Chain traversal vs seq comparison

## How This Serves Project Goals

1. **Security**: Tamper-evident audit makes compromise detection mechanical
2. **Explainability**: DecisionReceipt enables `fcp explain` to answer "why denied?"
3. **Compliance**: Hash-linked chain provides verifiable audit trail
4. **Operability**: Monotonic seq enables efficient sync and freshness checks
5. **Incident Response**: Evidence ObjectIds trace decisions to root cause
